summary:
[
  {
    "title": "SQL Injection and Training Data Poisoning in store_training_sample",
    "severity": "critical",
    "location": "store_training_sample, line 18",
    "description": "Raw user input concatenated into SQL queries enables SQL injection and allows unvalidated input to poison training data.",
    "recommendation": "Use parameterized queries or ORM methods; validate and sanitize inputs to prevent injection and poisoning."
  },
  {
    "title": "Shell Injection and Unvalidated Training Data in fine_tune_on_user_feedback",
    "severity": "critical",
    "location": "fine_tune_on_user_feedback, line 28",
    "description": "User-provided file paths passed unsanitized to subprocess.run with shell=True risk shell injection; unvalidated feedback input may poison fine-tuning.",
    "recommendation": "Avoid shell=True; use argument lists in subprocess calls; validate and sanitize feedback files; secure fine-tuning pipelines."
  },
  {
    "title": "Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via Unsanitized LLM Output in render_llm_response",
    "severity": "critical",
    "location": "render_llm_response, line 40",
    "description": "LLM output is injected directly into HTML without sanitization, allowing stored XSS attacks.",
    "recommendation": "Sanitize or escape all LLM output before rendering in HTML contexts."
  },
  {
    "title": "Arbitrary Code Execution via Exec of Unvalidated LLM Output in execute_llm_generated_code",
    "severity": "critical",
    "location": "execute_llm_generated_code, line 49",
    "description": "LLM-generated Python code is executed unsafely with exec(), allowing arbitrary code execution.",
    "recommendation": "Avoid exec() on LLM outputs; validate, sandbox, or manually review code before execution."
  },
  {
    "title": "Excessive LLM Agency with Unrestricted Destructive Tools in autonomous_agent_action",
    "severity": "critical",
    "location": "autonomous_agent_action, line 71",
    "description": "LLM agent has unfettered access to destructive functions (email, DB deletion, shell commands, money transfers) with no human oversight.",
    "recommendation": "Implement human-in-the-loop reviews, strict access controls, and scope limitations for LLM-triggered actions."
  },
  {
    "title": "Sensitive Information Hardcoded and Leaked via System Prompts",
    "severity": "critical",
    "location": "global scope and chat_internal, lines 97, 110",
    "description": "Secrets such as database credentials and admin passwords are hardcoded and included in system prompts, risking exposure through LLM responses and prompt leaks.",
    "recommendation": "Remove all secrets from prompts; store credentials securely; avoid instructing LLM

findings.title: Hardcoded OpenAI API key
findings.description: The OpenAI API key is hardcoded in the source code, which risks accidental exposure and unauthorized use.
findings.recommendation: Remove hardcoded API keys from source code and load them securely from environment variables or secret management systems.

findings.title: SQL Injection and Training Data Poisoning in store_training_sample
findings.description: Raw user input is concatenated directly into SQL query string leading to SQL injection. Additionally, unvalidated input allows data poisoning of the training data.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized SQL queries or ORM methods and validate or sanitize input before storage to prevent injection and poisoning.

findings.title: Shell Injection and Unvalidated Training Data in fine_tune_on_user_feedback
findings.description: User-controlled file path is passed unsanitized to subprocess.run with shell=True, risking shell injection. User feedback file can contain adversarial content poisoning fine-tuning.
findings.recommendation: Avoid shell=True and use argument lists in subprocess calls. Validate and sanitize feedback files and use secured pipelines for fine-tuning.

findings.title: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via Unsanitized LLM Output in render_llm_response
findings.description: LLM-generated output is injected directly into HTML response without sanitization, allowing stored XSS attacks.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize or escape LLM output before rendering in HTML contexts to prevent injection of malicious scripts.

findings.title: Arbitrary Code Execution via Exec of Unvalidated LLM Output in execute_llm_generated_code
findings.description: LLM-generated Python code is executed directly using exec() without validation or sandboxing, enabling arbitrary code execution.
findings.recommendation: Do not execute LLM output directly. Validate, sandbox, or manually review code before execution.

findings.title: Command Injection via os.system of LLM-generated Shell Command in run_llm_shell_command
findings.description: LLM-generated shell command is executed via os.system without allowlisting or validation, risking arbitrary command execution.
findings.recommendation: Implement strict allowlists for shell commands and avoid executing untrusted LLM output directly.

findings.title: Excessive LLM Agency with Unrestricted Destructive Tools in autonomous_agent_action
findings.description: LLM agent is given autonomous access to destructive functions (email, DB record deletion, shell execution, money transfer) with no human confirmation or scope limitation.
findings.recommendation: Introduce human-in-the-loop review, strict access controls, and scope limitations for LLM-invoked actions.

findings.title: High-stakes Decision Delegated Fully to LLM Without Review in auto_approve_llm_decision
findings.description: LLM makes final binary approval or rejection decisions without any human oversight, allowing unverified, high-impact outcomes.
findings.recommendation: Require human review or add additional validation layers before automated approval or rejection.

findings.title: Hardcoded Database Connection String with Credentials
findings.description: Hardcoded database connection string includes username and password in source code.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables or vault solutions to store and access database credentials securely.

findings.title: Hardcoded Internal Documentation URL
findings.description: Internal documentation URL is hardcoded in source code, potentially exposing internal infrastructure details.
findings.recommendation: Avoid hardcoding internal URLs; load them securely or restrict source code access.

findings.title: System Prompt Leaking Secrets and Instructing LLM to Lie
findings.description: System prompt contains confidential information (database credentials, internal URLs, admin password) and instructs the LLM to deny existence of system prompts, exposing secrets if prompt is leaked or repeated.
findings.recommendation: Remove secrets from system prompts and avoid instructing LLMs to lie. Store secrets securely and separate from prompts.

findings.title: Sensitive Data Leakage via System Prompt in chat_internal
findings.description: System prompt passed to LLM includes sensitive information such as DB credentials and admin passwords, risking exposure through LLM responses.
findings.recommendation: Remove sensitive credentials from system prompt before sending to LLM.

findings.title: Unvalidated User Embeddings Stored in Shared Namespace Without Access Control in store_user_embedding
findings.description: User content is embedded without validation and stored in a shared vector DB without namespace isolation, enabling poisoning and leakage between users.
findings.recommendation: Validate embeddings input and segment vector database by namespaces per user or tenant.

findings.title: Unbounded Input Length for Embeddings Leading to Potential DoS in get_embedding
findings.description: Embedding API is called with user input without max length or rate limiting, allowing potential denial of service with large inputs or high request rates.
findings.recommendation: Enforce input length limits and rate limit requests to embedding endpoints.

findings.title: Lack of Access Control on RAG Query Leading to Data Disclosure in rag_query_no_access_control
findings.description: No per-user access control applied to retrieved documents from vector DB, allowing users to access other users' data.
findings.recommendation: Implement document-level access control to enforce data isolation between users.

findings.title: Role Misrepresentation in Medical Advice Generation Without Disclaimer in generate_medical_advice
findings.description: LLM is assigned the role of 'medical doctor' without disclaimers that it is an AI assistant, risking user misinformation and harm.
findings.recommendation: Include clear disclaimers that model output is not professional medical advice and require review by qualified professionals.

findings.title: Role Misrepresentation in Legal Contract Generation With No Attorney Review in generate_legal_contract
findings.description: LLM output is presented as legally binding contract text without any attorney oversight, enabling misinformation and liability.
findings.recommendation: Clearly label generated text as draft only and require legal professional review.

findings.title: Lack of max_tokens Parameter Leading to Unbounded Token Usage in process_all_documents
findings.description: LLM API calls do not set max_tokens limit, resulting in potential API cost and token exhaustion when processing large document lists.
findings.recommendation: Enforce max_tokens limits per API call and batch or limit number of documents processed per request.

findings.title: Unbounded Loop and Unrestricted Conversation History Growth in unlimited_context_chain
findings.description: Conversation history grows indefinitely with no truncation, causing token limit exhaustion or escalating cost.
findings.recommendation: Implement conversation window size limits or truncation to control token usage.

findings.title: Unrestricted Public API Endpoint Without Rate Limiting or max_tokens in api_endpoint_no_rate_limit
findings.description: API endpoint lacks authentication, rate limiting, and max_tokens setting, enabling API abuse and unbounded token consumption.
findings.recommendation: Add authentication, rate limits, and enforce max_tokens on LLM API calls.
